edges.rel: grants life once a year to

beats.title: Table of Contents of Bhavishya Purana Part 1
beats.summary: This is a table of contents for the Bhavishya Purana (part 1), listing topics such as creation, dynasties, vratas (vows), and rituals. It includes an index of chapters with page numbers and mentions of kings, sages, and deities.

beats.title: Beginning of the Bhavishya Purana Recitation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana (Part 2) is a table of contents listing numerous vratas (religious observances), their procedures, and fruits. It also includes the beginning of the main narrative with King Shatānīka requesting sage Sumantu to recite the Purana, followed by a description of the Purana's structure and the creation of the universe.

beats.title: Cosmic Creation and the Glory of Brahmins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the creation of the universe by Brahma, the origin of the four Vedas, the four varnas (social classes), the measurement of time (from moments to kalpas), the four yugas (ages), the importance of Brahmins, and the forty-eight samskaras (sacraments) that lead to liberation.

beats.title: The Story of Dhanabardhana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various samskaras (sacraments) and rituals for different varnas (castes), including naming, tonsure, upanayana (sacred thread ceremony), and rules for eating and achamana (sipping water). It also narrates a story about a wealthy man named Dhanabardhana who interrupted his meal and suffered consequences.

beats.title: Duties of Brahmins and Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the rituals and duties of brahmins, including the sacred thread ceremony (upanayana), the importance of Gayatri mantra, sandhya worship, respect for teachers, and the consequences of neglecting Vedic study.

beats.title: Duties of a Brahmachari and Auspicious Signs of Women
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the duties and conduct of a brahmachari (celibate student), including rules for begging alms, respect for the guru, and the importance of the Gayatri mantra. It also describes auspicious and inauspicious physical characteristics of women for marriage, as narrated by Brahma to the sages.

beats.title: Characteristics of a Wife and Rules of Marriage
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the characteristics of an ideal wife, including physical signs (such as breasts, chin, hair, hands, nails, lines on hands, neck, face, lips, teeth, nose, eyes, ears, forehead, hair, voice, gait, and skin color) that indicate good fortune, as well as signs of misfortune. It also covers the importance of wealth before marriage, the necessity of marriage for a householder, the types of marriages (eight kinds), and rules for choosing a bride from appropriate castes.

beats.title: Discourse on Dharma and Marriage
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses various types of marriages (Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, Pishacha) and their consequences, the importance of righteous conduct (dharma), the duties of householders, protection of women, and the significance of following Vedic and Smriti injunctions. It also describes ideal locations for dwelling and the behavior towards wives and servants.

beats.title: Duties of Women and Household Management
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the conduct and duties of women, especially pativratas (devoted wives), and provides practical advice on household management, including storage of grains, utensils, and animal husbandry. It also covers the proper behavior of a wife towards her husband and in-laws, and the importance of cleanliness and organization in the home.

beats.title: Duties of Women and Household Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the duties and conduct of women, especially towards their husbands and co-wives, the importance of household rituals (pancha-mahayajnas), and the rules for fasting and eating on specific tithis. It also includes a dialogue between King Shatānīka and Sage Sumantu about the necessity of performing these rituals.

beats.title: The Bhṛtipadā Vow and Brahmā Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the Bhṛtipadā vow, its benefits, and the worship of Brahmā. It details the ritual procedures, including the construction of a yantra, nyāsa, and the use of the Gāyatrī mantra. The chapter also discusses the rewards of performing the vow, such as attaining Brahmaloka, and emphasizes the importance of Brahmā worship. It includes a dialogue between King Śatānīka and the sage Sumantu, and mentions the Ratha Yātrā of Brahmā in the month of Kārttika.

beats.title: The Story of Chyavana and Sukanya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the importance of the pratipada (first day) of the lunar fortnight, especially in Kartika, and narrates the story of King Sharyati, his daughter Sukanya, and the sage Chyavana. It details how Sukanya accidentally injured Chyavana's eyes, leading to her marriage to him, and later how the Ashvini Kumaras restored Chyavana's youth and beauty. The chapter concludes with Chyavana performing a sacrifice where he insists on giving a share to the Ashvini Kumaras despite Indra's opposition, ultimately forcing Indra to agree.

beats.title: Vratas and the Origin of Ganesha's Single Tusk
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with specific tithis (lunar days), including Phala Dvitiya, Tritiya, and Chaturthi. It narrates the story of the origin of Ganesha's single tusk and the conflict between Shiva, Brahma, Vishnu, Kartikeya, and Ganesha, culminating in the establishment of the Kapala vrata and the creation of Shvetakundali and Raktakundali.

beats.title: The Origin of Lakshanas and Ganesha Worship
beats.summary: King Shatanika asks Sage Sumantu about the origin of the characteristics (lakshanas) of men, which were created by Kartikeya but thrown into the ocean by Shiva in anger. Brahma later recounts these characteristics to Kartikeya. The chapter also describes the worship of Ganesha to remove obstacles, including rituals and mantras.

beats.title: Lakshanas of a Person
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various physical characteristics (lakshanas) of a person and their correlation with fortune, wealth, and social status. It covers features such as the navel, belly, hands, fingers, nails, palms, lines on the forehead, eyes, nose, ears, teeth, tongue, voice, and other bodily marks, indicating whether a person will be a king, wealthy, poor, or of low character.

beats.title: Lakshanas of Kings and Women
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the auspicious and inauspicious bodily signs (laksanas) of men and women, particularly focusing on kings and queens. It includes a dialogue between Kartikeya and Brahma, where Brahma explains the characteristics that indicate royalty, prosperity, and moral qualities based on physical features such as hair, eyes, forehead, and limbs. The chapter also categorizes women based on their traits and behaviors, linking them to divine or demonic natures.

beats.title: Worship of Ganesha and the Naga Panchami Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Ganesha, including the method of making his image, mantras, offerings, and the benefits of worship. It also narrates the story of the curse on the Nagas by their mother Kadru and the prophecy of their salvation through the sage Jaratkaru and his son Astika. The chapter concludes with instructions for the Naga Panchami vrata to appease snakes and ensure the welfare of ancestors who died from snakebite.

beats.title: Kashyapa and Gautama on Snakes and Venom
beats.summary: This chapter is a dialogue between the sages Kashyapa and Gautama about the nature of snakes, their venom, and the treatment of snakebites. It covers the origin of snakes, their anatomy, types of fangs, venom spread, and various herbal remedies for different stages of envenomation.

beats.title: Discourse on Snakes and Brahmanahood
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the classification of snakes (nagas) by caste (Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya, Shudra), their characteristics, venom effects, and treatments. It also describes the worship of nagas on specific lunar days (Panchami) and the benefits thereof. Additionally, it explores the nature of Brahmanahood through a dialogue between sages and Brahma, questioning whether it is determined by birth, deeds, or knowledge.

beats.title: Discourse on Caste and Kartikeya's Glory
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the nature of caste (varna) and Brahminhood, arguing that true Brahminhood is determined by qualities and actions, not birth or rituals. It cites examples of sages born from low origins who became Brahmins through tapas, and criticizes those who perform rites but lack virtue. The chapter also includes a section on Kartikeya worship and its benefits.

beats.title: Saptami Kalpa and the Glory of Surya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Saptami Kalpa, the origin of the Sun god, his marriage to Samjna and Chhaya, the birth of their children, the story of the Sun's form being trimmed by Vishvakarma, the importance of the Saptami vrata, and a dialogue between Krishna and Shamba about the supremacy of Surya Narayana and the method of his worship.

beats.title: Worship of Surya Narayana and the Chariot of the Sun
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana details the worship of Surya Narayana (the Sun God) through various rituals, including mantra recitation, nyasa, puja with red sandalwood and flowers, and homa. It describes the benefits of such worship, such as relief from disease, wealth, and liberation. The chapter also covers the construction and symbolism of Surya's chariot, the deities accompanying him in different months, and the story of Shamba learning the worship from Krishna. Additionally, it explains the Ratha Saptami vrata and the annual chariot festival.

beats.title: The Solar Chariot and the Rathayatra
beats.summary: This chapter describes the solar chariot of Surya Narayana, its divine attendants, the daily and yearly journey of the sun, the effects of the sun's colors, and the rituals of the Rathayatra (chariot festival) including its benefits. It also includes a dialogue between Rudra and Brahma about the propriety of moving a consecrated image.

beats.title: Rathayatra of Suryanarayana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of the Rathayatra (chariot festival) of Suryanarayana, including the construction of the chariot, the procession, the offerings to various deities, the significance of the festival, and the procedures for atonement if any mishap occurs. It also details the worship of the nine planets (Navagrahas) and the specific offerings for each deity.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 2 (part 9)
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and benefits of the Ratha Saptami and Magha Saptami vratas, including worship of Surya Narayana, chariot construction, and the fruits of these observances for different castes and genders. It also includes dialogues between Brahma, Rudra, and sages, and the story of Tandi's liberation from Brahma-hatya through Surya worship.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 2 (part 10)
beats.summary: Tandi asks Surya Narayana for an easy path to liberation. Surya Narayana instructs him on devotion, worship, and the importance of initiation (diksha). Tandi then goes to Brahma, who elaborates on the Phala Saptami vrata, its rules, and benefits. The chapter details the worship of Surya Narayana, the significance of fasting, and the rewards of the vrata.

beats.title: The Glory of Surya Narayana Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Surya Narayana vrata, including rules for conduct, worship procedures, and the story of Shamba's cure from leprosy through Surya worship. It also includes a dialogue between Brahma and Yajnavalkya about the supremacy of Surya, and a list of flowers and offerings pleasing to Surya.

beats.title: Samba's Leprosy and Cure by Surya Worship
beats.summary: This chapter describes various rituals, vows (vratas), and dream interpretations associated with Surya (Sun god) worship, including the Sarshapa Saptami vrata. It also narrates the story of Samba, son of Krishna, who was cursed by his father to become a leper, and later cured by worshipping Surya. The chapter includes a list of Surya's names and concludes with instructions for recitation.

beats.title: Samba's Redemption through Surya Worship
beats.summary: Samba, cursed by Krishna and Durvasa, seeks redemption. Narada advises him to worship Suryanarayana. The chapter describes Surya's glory, his twelve forms, and the cosmic significance of the sun god.

beats.title: The Glory of Surya Narayana and the Story of Samjna and Chhaya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the nature of Surya Narayana (the Sun god), his rays, his forms as various deities, and his role in time and creation. It narrates the story of Surya's wives Samjna and Chhaya, their children, and the events leading to Surya's form being trimmed by Vishvakarma, resulting in the birth of Revanta and the Ashvins.

beats.title: The Glory of Surya and the Saptami Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the lineage and attributes of Surya Narayana (the Sun god), his children including Yama, Yamuna, Ashwini Kumaras, Revanta, and others. It details the importance of worshipping Surya on specific days, especially Sundays and the seventh lunar day (Saptami), with various rituals, fasting, and offerings. The chapter also explains the different types of Saptami (like Vijaya Saptami) and their benefits, as well as the significance of specific lunar days and planetary combinations for worship.

beats.title: Vratas for Suryanarayana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) dedicated to Suryanarayana (the Sun God), detailing the specific days, rituals, offerings, and benefits for each. It covers vratas like Kamadaha, Patrada, Vijaya, Adityamimukha, Hridaya, Rogahara, and others, emphasizing the importance of fasting, worship, donations, and the recitation of mantras. The chapter also discusses the merits of performing rituals during specific astronomical events such as solstices, equinoxes, and eclipses.

beats.title: The Glory of Puranic Recitation in Surya Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the merits of worshiping Suryanarayana (the Sun God), especially through offerings, recitation of Puranas, and building temples. It narrates a story where Brahma asks Suryanarayana about two men who received special honor, and Suryanarayana explains that they were a devotee who built a temple and a Brahmin who listened to Puranic stories. The chapter emphasizes the superiority of Puranic recitation over other rituals.

beats.title: Saptami Vrata Mahatmya
beats.summary: This chapter describes various Saptami (seventh lunar day) vratas (vows) dedicated to Suryanarayana (Sun god). It details the procedures, offerings, and fruits of different Saptamis such as Jaya, Jayanti, Vijaya, Mahajaya, Nanda, and Bhadra Saptami. The chapter emphasizes the importance of fasting, worship with specific flowers and incense, and the benefits of wealth, fame, progeny, health, and ultimate liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Tithis and Nakshatras with Suryanarayana as Supreme
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the worship of various deities on specific tithis (lunar days) and nakshatras (lunar mansions), emphasizing the supremacy of Suryanarayana (the Sun god). It details the fruits of worshiping each tithi's presiding deity, the benefits of nakshatra worship, and the importance of devotion, fasting, and temple construction for Suryanarayana. The chapter concludes with instructions for the Saptami (seventh day) fast in the bright half of Phalguna.

beats.title: Saptami Vrata Mahatmya
beats.summary: This chapter describes various Saptami (seventh day) vows dedicated to Suryanarayana, including their procedures, benefits, and associated rituals. It covers different Saptamis like Jaya, Vijaya, Jayanti, and others, and explains the fruits of observing these vows, such as attaining the solar world, wealth, health, and liberation from sins.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 2 (part 21)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the worship of Suryanarayana (the Sun God). It discusses the importance of purity in heart, speech, and action for worship. It details the rituals for Surya's worship, including fasting on Margashirsha Saptami when it falls on a Sunday, offering various items, and the benefits of building a temple and installing an image. The story of King Satrajit and his queen is narrated, where the queen's devotion to Surya leads to prosperity. The chapter also includes a dialogue between Koshala and Gotami about the fruits of Surya worship, and lists acceptable and unacceptable offerings.

beats.title: The Vow of Suryanarayana and the Origin of Bhojakas
beats.summary: King Kubalashva and his queen, after hearing the story of their past lives from the sage Paravasu, learn about the importance of worshipping Suryanarayana. Paravasu explains their previous birth as a cruel king and his devoted wife, who served in a temple and attained wealth. He then instructs them on the three-month vow (trimasik vrata) in Kartika, Margashirsha, and Pausha to cleanse sins. The king asks about the proper recipient of offerings, leading to a discourse on the Bhojaka priests, their origin from Suryanarayana's body, and their duties.

beats.title: The Glory of Lighting Lamps for Sūryanārāyaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter of the Bhavishya Purana discusses the importance of fasting on Shamī and Saṅkrānti, the worship of Sūryanārāyaṇa, the merit of lighting lamps in his temple, especially in Kārtika month, and the consequences of stealing lamps. It includes stories of a Brahmin named Bhadra who lights lamps, a thief who steals ornaments but is saved by lighting a lamp, and a discourse on the qualities of a Vivasvat devotee. The chapter also covers the origin of deities from Sūryanārāyaṇa's rays and the necessity of becoming a Vivasvat to worship him.

beats.title: The Softening of Sūrya's Effulgence
beats.summary: Sage Sumantu narrates to King Shatānīka the story of Sūrya Nārāyaṇa (the Sun God). He describes how Brahmā and Viṣṇu advised Sūrya to have his fierce form softened by Viśvakarmā. The gods and sages praise Sūrya, and Viśvakarmā reduces his effulgence, creating divine weapons from the excess. The chapter emphasizes the worship of Sūrya Nārāyaṇa for attaining desires and liberation.

beats.title: The Cosmic Form of Suryanarayana and Samba's Penance
beats.summary: This chapter describes the cosmic form of Suryanarayana, his attendants, the structure of the universe including various lokas, mountains, oceans, and the genealogy of gods, sages, and Manus. It also narrates the story of Samba's penance to cure his disease by worshiping Suryanarayana.

beats.title: Samba's Penance and the Installation of Surya's Image
beats.summary: Samba performs penance and receives a boon from Surya Narayana, who cures his leprosy and grants him a divine form. Surya instructs Samba to install an image of the Sun god on the banks of the Chandrabhaga river. Samba later receives a 21-name stotra from Surya. After obtaining the boon, Samba finds a self-manifested image of Surya in the river and installs it in Mitravana. Narada then advises Samba on the construction of temples and installation procedures, describing various types of prasada (temples) and their dimensions.

beats.title: Instructions on Temple Construction and Image Making
beats.summary: Narada instructs Samba on the construction of a temple for Surya Narayana, including site selection, ground testing, temple layout, placement of deities, types of materials for the image, characteristics of a good tree for carving, auspicious and inauspicious marks on wood, dimensions and proportions of the image, and effects of defects in the image.

beats.title: Installation of Surya Narayana
beats.summary: Narada instructs Samba on the installation and worship of Surya Narayana, describing the deity's cosmic form, auspicious times, construction of a mandapa, preparation of the image, bathing rituals with mantras, and the adhivasa ceremony. The chapter details the qualifications of the priest and the benefits of the worship.

beats.title: Installation of Suryanarayana and Flag Raising
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals for installing an image of Suryanarayana (Sun god), including worship of the ten directional deities, offering oblations, and the benefits of such installation. It also details the construction and raising of a flag (dhvaja) on a temple, including specifications for the flagpole and banner, the associated mantras, and the merits of the act.

beats.title: The Origin of the Maga Brahmins and Their Installation as Sun Priests
beats.summary: Samba seeks a priest for the Sun temple he built. After being refused by local Brahmins, he learns from Goramukha about the Maga Brahmins from Shaka-dvipa, who are descendants of Surya Narayana and a Naga princess. Samba brings eighteen Maga youths to Jambu-dvipa, establishes them as priests, and later inquires about their customs from Vyasa, who explains their origins, rituals, and status.

beats.title: The Origin and Worship of the Avyanga and the Glory of the Bhojakas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and significance of the 'avyanga' (a sacred thread or ornament) worn by the Bhojaka priests, who are descendants of Samba and Shakya women. It details the ritual worship of Suryanarayana, including the proper times, mantras, and offerings. The chapter also includes a discourse by Vyasa on the three kalas (energies) of the Sun and the nature of the supreme self.

beats.title: The Aditya Hridaya Stotra and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the practice of detachment, the purification of the mind through pranayama and meditation, and the worship of Suryanarayana (the Sun God). It includes the Aditya Hridaya Stotra, a hymn to the Sun, with detailed instructions for nyasa (ritual placement) and meditation. The chapter also recounts how Arjuna asked Krishna about the stotra during the Kurukshetra war, and Krishna taught it to him. The benefits of reciting the stotra are enumerated, including freedom from sins, diseases, and enemies.

beats.title: The Glory of the Sun and the Aditya Hridaya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the twelve Adityas (sons of Aditi and Kashyapa), their nature as manifestations of Vishnu, Shiva, and Brahma, and the supreme deity Surya (the Sun). It extols the virtues of reciting the Aditya Hridaya (the heart of the Sun) for purification, healing, and liberation from sins and diseases. The text includes a detailed mantra, instructions for worship, and promises of various benefits such as wealth, health, and ultimate union with the divine.

beats.title: Aditya Hridaya and Prophecy of Kali Yuga Kings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana contains an Aditya Hridaya stotra (hymn to the Sun) and a prophecy of future kings in Kali Yuga, followed by a dialogue between King Yudhishthira and sages including Vyasa and Krishna about the nature of the universe.

beats.title: Vishnu's Maya Shown to Narada
beats.summary: This chapter describes the process of creation from primordial Prakriti, the formation of the cosmic egg, the seven continents and oceans, the seven netherworlds, the Manvantaras, and the nature of the universe. It then narrates a story of Vishnu showing his Maya to Narada: Vishnu and Narada travel as brahmins, encounter a prostitute and a farmer, and later Narada becomes a beautiful woman who marries King Taladhvaja, bears many sons who eventually destroy each other, revealing the illusory nature of the world.

beats.title: The Illusion of Vishnu and the Suffering of Life
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the nature of fate, the illusion (maya) of Vishnu, the cycle of birth and death, the suffering in the womb and throughout life, and the inevitability of death. It includes a story of Narada being transformed into a woman by Vishnu's maya and then restored. The chapter emphasizes the futility of worldly attachments and the importance of seeking liberation.

beats.title: Discourse on Suffering and Sin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the pervasive nature of suffering in worldly life, emphasizing that wealth, seasons, family, and even kingship are sources of distress. It categorizes sins into mental, verbal, and physical types, detailing major sins (mahapataka) and minor sins (upapataka), their consequences in hell, and the role of Yama's judgment. The text also describes the contrasting paths of the virtuous and sinful after death, highlighting the importance of righteous deeds and charity, especially food donation.

beats.title: The Judgement of Yama and the Torments of Hell
beats.summary: This chapter describes the journey of souls to Yama's realm after death, where they are judged by Yama and Chitragupta. It details the punishments for various sins, including torture in multiple hells, and emphasizes the importance of virtuous deeds to avoid such suffering. The chapter also discusses the cycle of rebirth and the rarity of human birth, urging readers to perform righteous actions.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 2 (part 39)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the importance of avoiding sin and performing virtuous deeds, especially devotion to Shiva. It narrates stories illustrating the power of vows (vratas) such as the Shakatavrata, Tilakavrata, Ashokavrata, and Kokilavrata, which grant blessings, protection from evil, and ultimate liberation. The chapter also includes instructions for performing these vows and their associated rituals.

beats.title: The Vratas and the Tale of King Svarnasthivi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vratas (religious observances), including the Kokila vrata, a vrata for Goddess Gauri, a vrata for Lord Shiva, and the Chaturmasya vrata. It also narrates the story of King Satyavrata (or Svarnasthivi) and his son, who was killed by thieves but revived by Narada's grace. The chapter emphasizes the benefits of these vratas, such as attaining beauty, wealth, longevity, and liberation.

beats.title: Vratas for Marital Bliss and Prosperity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) for women and men to ensure marital bliss, avoid widowhood, and attain prosperity. It includes the Ashunya Shayana (non-empty bed) vrata on Krishna Dwitiya, the Gopada vrata on Bhadra Shukla Dwitiya, the Harakali vrata, and a twelve-month vrata for goddess worship. The narrative involves dialogues between King Yudhishthira and Lord Krishna, and between Parvati and Shiva, explaining the origins and benefits of these rituals.

beats.title: The Lalita Vrata and the Origin of Saubhagya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Lalita Vrata (also called Umamaheshvara Vrata) performed by women for marital bliss, beauty, and prosperity. It includes the story of Sati/Lalita, daughter of Daksha, who married Shiva. The vrata involves monthly worship of Shiva and Parvati with specific flowers and offerings, culminating in an annual udyapana. The chapter also recounts the origin of saubhagya (good fortune) from the cosmic dissolution and its distribution by Daksha.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 2 (part 43)
beats.summary: This chapter describes two vratas (religious observances) dedicated to the goddess Lalita/Parvati: the 'Bhagyashayana' vrata and the 'Kalyani' vrata. It details the rituals, mantras, offerings, and benefits for each, including the worship of Shiva-Parvati, the use of specific flowers and items, and the importance of honoring Brahmin couples. The chapter also includes a dialogue between King Yudhishthira and Lord Krishna about the significance of these vratas.

beats.title: Vratas and the Origin of Angaraka
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vratas (vows) dedicated to goddess Parvati and other deities, focusing on the Anantya Vrata and Akshaya Tritiya. It includes rituals, offerings, and the benefits of observing these vratas, such as attaining wealth, progeny, and liberation. The chapter also narrates the story of King Dharman and the origin of Angaraka (Mars) from Shiva's blood.

beats.title: Rituals and Vows for Worship
beats.summary: This chapter describes various rituals and vows (vratas) for worshiping deities like Bhoma (Mars), Vinayaka (Ganesha), and Saraswati, including the Angaraka Chaturthi vrata, Vinayaka puja for removing obstacles, Shanti vrata for peace, and Saraswati vrata for wisdom. It also includes instructions for bathing, mantra recitation, and offerings.

beats.title: Nag Panchami and Shri Panchami
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin of the Nag Panchami festival, the story of Kadru's curse on the Nagas, and the promise of salvation through Astika. It also narrates the origin of the Shri Panchami vrata, the churning of the ocean, and the emergence of Lakshmi. The chapter provides detailed rituals for both Nag Panchami and Shri Panchami, emphasizing their benefits.

beats.title: Vratas of Shashthi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with the sixth lunar day (Shashthi) in different months. It includes the Vishoka Shashthi, Mandara Shashthi, Kamala Shashthi, and a Shashthi vrata for women seeking beauty and children. The rituals involve fasting, worship of Surya (Sun god) with red flowers or Mandara (crown flower) and specific mantras, donations to Brahmins, and culminate in rewards such as freedom from sorrow, attainment of desires, and residence in celestial realms.

beats.title: Vows and Rebirth: The Story of Chandraprabha and Bhushana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Kartikeya (Skanda) and the observance of the Vijaya Saptami vrata (fast on the seventh lunar day of the bright fortnight associated with Sunday). It also narrates the story of Queen Chandraprabha and the wife of the priest (Bhushana) who performed a vow for Shiva and Parvati, leading to their rebirth and the importance of completing vows with udyapana (concluding ceremony).

beats.title: Achala Saptami Vrata and the Story of Chandramati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with the seventh lunar day (saptami), especially in the month of Magha. It includes the story of a prostitute named Chandramati who, through the advice of sage Vashishtha, performs the Achala Saptami vrata and attains liberation. The chapter also details the rituals for Surya Narayana worship, including the use of red flowers, offerings, and mantras, and emphasizes the benefits of these observances for health, wealth, and progeny.

beats.title: The Glories of Budhashtami and Janmashtami
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory and ritual of Budhashtami (Budha Ashtami) and Janmashtami (Krishna's birth). It includes the story of King Ilana who became a woman in a forest, married Budha, and fathered Pururavas. It also narrates the tale of Shyamala, daughter of Umila, who married Dharmaraja and saw her mother's suffering in seven hells. The chapter then details the vrata (vow) for Budhashtami and Janmashtami, including the construction of a tableau of Krishna's birth, worship of deities, and the benefits of observing these vows.

beats.title: The Vratas of Durvashtami and Krishna Ashtami and the Story of Dattatreya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and significance of the Durvashtami and Krishna Ashtami vratas, including the story of Dattatreya's birth, his protection of the gods from the demon Jambha, and his boon to King Kartavirya Arjuna. It also details the rituals and benefits of observing these vratas.

beats.title: The Boons of Kartavirya Arjuna and the Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the story of King Kartavirya Arjuna, who served the sage Dattatreya and received boons including a thousand arms, invincibility in battle, and a righteous kingdom. It details his exploits, including capturing Ravana and offering the earth to Agni. The chapter then shifts to the narration of the Anaghashtami vrata (fast) performed by Kartavirya, which grants all desires. It also describes the Harivara vrata (worship of Shiva and Vishnu) and the Shrivriksha vrata (worship of the bilva tree as Lakshmi). Finally, it recounts the battle between the gods and the demon Raktasura, and the appearance of the goddess with the nine Durgas to protect the gods.

beats.title: Vows and Redemption
beats.summary: This chapter describes various rituals and vows (vrata) dedicated to the goddess (Devi) and Vishnu, including the Navami vrata, Ulka Navami, and Dvadashi vrata. It also narrates the story of King Yashodhvaja's multiple births due to killing a Brahmin, and the importance of the Taraka Dvadashi vrata. The chapter includes instructions for worship, offerings, and the benefits of observing these vows.

beats.title: Aranya Dwadashi and Rainy Season Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes two vratas (vows): the Aranya Dwadashi vrata, which was performed by Sita in the forest on Rama's command, and a vrata for the rainy season involving offerings to Chandra and Rohini. The Aranya Dwadashi involves fasting, worship of Vishnu with twelve names, feeding Brahmins in a forest, and results in attaining Shwetadweepa and liberation. The rainy season vrata involves feeding fish with urad flour dumplings and worshiping Chandra with Rohini, leading to various heavenly realms.

beats.title: The Glory of Cows and the Go-vatsa Dvadashi Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of cows and the Go-vatsa Dvadashi vrata. It includes the story of King Uttanapada and his queens, the appearance of Shiva and Parvati as a Brahmin couple and a lion, and the establishment of the vrata. The chapter also lists the deities residing in different parts of a cow's body and explains the benefits of the vrata.

beats.title: The Story of King Prajapala and Ravana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and observances of Chaturmasya (four-month period) and the Vishnu Shayana (sleeping) and Utthana (awakening) ceremonies. It details the rules for fasting, offerings, and the benefits of various abstinences. It also narrates the story of King Prajapala, who controlled diseases and demons, and his encounter with Ravana. The chapter concludes with the Bhishma Panchaka vow.

beats.title: Vratas and the Vamana Avatar
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vratas (vows) and their associated rituals, including the Karttika Shukla Ekadashi, the Matsya Dvadashi, the Vamana Dvadashi, and others. It includes instructions for worship, fasting, and donations, as well as stories about King Bhima, King Yudhishthira, and the Vamana avatar. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of these vratas and the importance of devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: The Power of Dwadashi Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vows (vratas) related to Dwadashi, especially the Govinda Dwadashi, Akhanda Dwadashi, and Manoratha Dwadashi. It explains the rituals, benefits, and the importance of avoiding defects (vaikalpa) in observances. The story of the merchant Shibhadra who became a preta (ghost) due to his sins and was liberated by the power of Dwadashi vrata is narrated. The chapter also discusses the significance of til (sesame) donations and the Sukrita Dwadashi vrata.

beats.title: The Twelve Dvadashi Vratas and the Avatars of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the vrata (vows) for each of the twelve dvadashis (twelfth lunar days) in the Hindu calendar, associated with the ten avatars of Vishnu (Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, and Kalki). It details the rituals, mantras, and benefits of observing these fasts, including liberation from sins, attainment of heaven, and worldly prosperity. The chapter also includes a dialogue between King Yudhishthira and Lord Krishna about the efficacy of these vows in the Kali Yuga.

beats.title: Vratas and Dana for Vishnu's Avatars
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vratas (vows) and dana (donations) associated with specific tithis (lunar days), particularly the dvadashi (twelfth day) of bright fortnights. It details rituals for worshiping Vishnu's avatars (Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Balarama, Buddha, Kalki) and the benefits of performing these rites, including liberation from sins, attainment of desires, and reaching Vishnu's abode. The chapter also covers the construction and donation of gudadhenu (jaggery cow) and other dhenus, and the vrata called Vishokadvadashi which removes sorrow.

beats.title: Glories of Dvadashi and Trayodashi Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of the Dvadashi and Trayodashi vratas (vows), including the story of a hunter who attained kingship by offering lotuses to a deity, the story of Diti performing a vow to obtain powerful sons, and the benefits of worshipping the goddess Mangala and the sun god. It also includes instructions for various rituals and vows.

beats.title: Vows to Avoid Yama and the Birth of Skanda
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vows (vrata) to avoid Yama's punishment and attain heavenly realms. It includes the Yama-darshana vrata, Ananga Trayodashi vrata, and Kadali vrata, with detailed procedures and benefits. It also narrates the story of Tarakasura's defeat by Skanda, born from Shiva's seed, and the role of Agni.

beats.title: The Hiding of Agni and the Dispute of Sages
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the hiding of Agni, the search by the gods, encounters with various beings, the birth of Kumara (Skanda) from Shiva's seed, the dispute between Utathya and Angira, the role of Surya, and the rituals of Chaturdashi and Shravanika vrata.

beats.title: The Vow of Shivachaturdashi and the Tale of Chandra and Tara
beats.summary: This chapter describes the vow (vrata) of Shivachaturdashi, including its procedure, benefits, and the story of Chandra's abduction of Tara, which led to a war between gods and demons, and the birth of Budha. It also covers the Phala-tyaga vow and the significance of Purnima.

beats.title: The Importance of Tithis and the Savitri Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the importance of certain tithis (lunar days) for performing rituals like snana (bathing), dana (charity), and puja (worship). It highlights the benefits of observing these tithis, especially the full moon days of Vaishakha, Kartika, and Magha, and the yugadi tithis (Vaishakha Shukla Tritiya, Kartika Shukla Navami, Bhadrapada Krishna Trayodashi, and Magha Purnima). The story of King Ashvapati and his daughter Savitri is introduced, explaining the origin of the Savitri vrata.

beats.title: The Story of Savitri and Satyavan
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Savitri and Satyavan, including Savitri's choice of husband, Narada's prophecy of Satyavan's early death, Savitri's unwavering devotion, her confrontation with Yama (Dharma Raja), and the boons she obtains. It also describes the Savitri Vrata and the Karttika Vrata, with instructions for their observance and the story of Queen Kalingabhadra and Yogalakshmi.

beats.title: Vratas for Purnima and the Ananta Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with full moon days (purnimas) and specific nakshatras, focusing on Karttiki, Phalguni, and other months. It includes rituals for worshipping deities like Krishna, Lakshmi, Janardana, and Ananta, with detailed procedures for bathing, offerings, mantra recitation, and charity. The story of Queen Shilaghana and the Ananta vrata is narrated, leading to the birth of a son named Kartavirya, who later worships Dattatreya.

beats.title: Ananta Vrata and Nakshatra Purusha Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Ananta Vrata and Nakshatra Purusha Vrata, including their procedures and benefits. It narrates the story of King Kartavirya, the sage Sambharayini, and the dialogue between Yudhishthira and Krishna about preserving wealth and attaining good form. The chapter also includes instructions for worshiping Vishnu and Shiva in the form of Nakshatra Purusha.

beats.title: Vows for Women and Prostitutes, and the Story of Krishna's Queens
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vows (vrata) and rituals, including the Nakshatra Purusha vrata, a vow for women to avoid widowhood, a vow for prostitutes' liberation, and a vow for Yama worship. It includes instructions for worship, mantras, and the benefits of performing these rites. The chapter also narrates a story of Krishna's queens who were cursed to become prostitutes and the means for their redemption.

beats.title: The Vratas for Planetary Peace and the Legend of Pippalada
beats.summary: This chapter describes various vratas (vows) and rituals to propitiate the nine planets (Navagrahas), especially Saturn (Shani), to alleviate their malefic effects. It includes the story of Pippalada, a sage who overcame Shani's affliction through penance and was blessed by Brahma and Vishnu. The chapter also details the worship of Bhadra (Vishthi), the daughter of Surya, and the Sankranti vrata.

beats.title: Bhadra Vrata and Agastya Charita
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and nature of Bhadra (Vishthi), a malevolent entity, and the method to propitiate her through a vrata. It then narrates the story of Sage Agastya: his birth from Mitra and Varuna, marriage to Lopamudra, destruction of demons Ilvala and Vatapi, drinking of the ocean, calming of the Vindhya mountain, and his various deeds. Finally, it prescribes the ritual for the Agastya vrata.

beats.title: Rituals and Vows for Worshiping Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various rituals and vows (vratas) for worshiping deities such as Chandra (Moon), Shukra (Venus), Brihaspati (Jupiter), Shiva, Vishnu, and Gauri. It includes instructions for offering arghya (libations) to the new moon, performing puja to Shukra and Brihaspati for prosperity, and observing vows like the Shila-vrata, Rudra-vrata, Panchaghata-vrata, and others for attaining specific benefits such as wealth, health, liberation, and residence in heavenly realms. The chapter also mentions the importance of donating items to Brahmins and reciting specific mantras.

beats.title: Enumeration of Vratas and Their Rewards
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes numerous vratas (religious observances) and their associated rituals, including fasting, donations, and worship of deities such as Shiva, Vishnu, Brahma, Gauri, Lakshmi, and others. Each vrata promises specific rewards like prosperity, beauty, liberation, or residence in various heavenly realms. The text emphasizes the importance of performing these vows with devotion and proper offerings to Brahmins.

beats.title: Vows and Their Fruits
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various vows (vrata) and their associated rituals, including fasting, donations, and bathing, with specific instructions for different lunar days and months. It emphasizes the benefits of observing these vows, such as attaining heavenly realms, liberation from sins, and blessings for ancestors. The chapter also discusses the importance of bathing in the month of Magha, the proper procedure for invoking the Ganga, and the classification of sins and their expiation.

beats.title: Rituals for Women, Eclipse Bath, and Deathbed Meditation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes three main topics: the Rudra-snana (a ritual bath for women to obtain sons and remove afflictions), the solar eclipse bath ritual for removing planetary afflictions, and the method of dying with mindfulness of Vishnu to attain liberation. It includes dialogues between Yudhishthira and Krishna, and between Agastya and Kartikeya.

beats.title: The Merits of Constructing Water Bodies and Planting Trees
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the construction and consecration of water bodies (tanks, wells, ponds) and tree planting. It details the rituals for establishing a tank (tadaga), including the selection of materials, construction of altars, invocation of deities, and the final consecration. The chapter also covers the establishment of wells (kupa) and the planting of trees, emphasizing the spiritual benefits and the importance of providing water and shade for travelers. It concludes with the merits of such acts, including liberation of ancestors and attainment of heaven.

beats.title: Rituals for Temple Establishment and Lamp Offering
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes rituals for the installation and worship of a deity (daksha), including the construction of a temple, the establishment of an image, and the performance of various rites such as homa, puja, and dipadana (lamp offering). It also narrates stories about King Trisanku, the sage Vishwamitra, and the importance of lamp offerings, especially during Kartika Purnima. The chapter emphasizes the merits of building temples, installing images, and performing acts of devotion like offering lamps, which lead to heavenly rewards.

beats.title: Origins of Holi, Damana Worship, and Dolotsava
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the origins and rituals of the Holi festival (Holika), the Dolotsava (swing festival), and the worship of the Damana plant. It begins with the story of King Yudhishthira asking Krishna about the Holi festival. Krishna narrates how the demoness Dhola (or Holika) was tormenting children, and through the advice of sage Vashishtha, the people performed a ritual with fire, obscene language, and child warriors to destroy her. The chapter also covers the story of Damana (a fragrant plant) cursed by Brahma but later blessed to be used in worship during spring. Finally, it describes the Dolotsava, where Shiva and Parvati enjoyed a swing, leading to cosmic disturbance, and the subsequent establishment of the swing festival as a meritorious practice.

beats.title: Spring Festivals and the Story of Kamadeva
beats.summary: This chapter describes the spring festival (Vasanta Utsava), the origin of the Ratha Yatra (chariot festival), and the story of Kamadeva's destruction and partial revival by Shiva. It includes instructions for various rituals and festivals to be performed in the spring season, such as the Dolotsava (swing festival), Ratha Yatra, and the worship of Kamadeva. The chapter also narrates how Narada visited Shiva, described the influence of spring, and how Shiva subdued the gods' intoxication and established the Ratha Yatra. Additionally, it recounts the story of Kamadeva's attempt to arouse Shiva's passion for Parvati, resulting in Kamadeva being burnt to ashes, and later being granted life once a year on the thirteenth day of the bright fortnight of Chaitra.

beats.title: The Origin of Matamata Worship and the Raksha Bandhan
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of the goddess Matamata (Bhadrakali) and the Raksha Bandhan ritual. It narrates the origin of two fierce beings from Shiva and Parvati, the story of Indra's defeat by demons and his subsequent victory due to a protective amulet tied by Shachi, and the significance of Maha Navami. The chapter also details the procedures for Raksha Bandhan and the worship of the goddess, including animal sacrifice.

beats.title: The Lohabhisara Ritual for Kings
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Lohabhisara ritual for kings, including the construction of a mandapa, worship of weapons and royal insignia with mantras, the story of the demon Loha, and the detailed procedure for the festival culminating in animal sacrifice and procession of the goddess's image.

beats.title: Rituals of the Goddess, Indra-dhvaja, and Koumudi Festival
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and festivals associated with the worship of the goddess (Devi) and the Indra-dhvaja (Indra's banner), as well as the observance of the Koumudi (Karttika) festival dedicated to King Bali. It includes instructions for setting up the banner, performing worship, and the benefits of these practices. The chapter also discusses the importance of the Karttika Krishna Chaturdashi and Amavasya tithis, with rituals for pacifying planets and ensuring prosperity.

beats.title: King Samvarana Seeks Sage Sanaka's Advice on Planetary Pacification
beats.summary: This chapter describes the procedure for performing the Navagraha Shanti (pacification of the nine planets) ritual, including the construction of the altar, placement of deities, offerings, homa (fire sacrifice), meditation on the planetary deities, and the benefits of performing the ritual. It also includes a narrative about King Samvarana seeking advice from Sage Sanaka on how to pacify planetary afflictions.

beats.title: Kotihoma and Mahashanti Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter describes the procedure for the Kotihoma (ten million oblations) sacrifice and the Mahashanti (great peace) ritual to counteract various portents and calamities. It includes instructions for constructing the sacrificial altar, invoking deities, performing homas, and the benefits of these rites. The chapter also discusses the importance of charity and the fate of those who do not give.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 3 (part 31)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the rules and merits of donating cows (go-dana), sesame cows (tila-dhenu), and water pots (jala-dhenu). It includes instructions on the characteristics of suitable cows and recipients, the ritual procedure, and the benefits of such donations, including liberation from sins and attainment of heaven. The chapter also narrates the story of sage Mandagala visiting Yama's realm and witnessing the power of jala-dhenu donation.

beats.title: The Glories of Donating Symbolic Cows
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of Vishnu's maya, the importance of devotion to Vishnu, and the merits of donating various symbolic cows (ghrita-dhenu, lavana-dhenu, suvarna-dhenu, ratna-dhenu, and ubhayamukhi-dhenu). It includes instructions for constructing and donating these cows, along with associated mantras and benefits.

beats.title: The Merits of Various Donations
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the merits and procedures of various donations: the bull (Vrishabha) donation, buffalo (Mahishi) donation, sheep (Meshi) donation, and land (Bhumi) donation. It emphasizes the spiritual benefits, including liberation from sins, attainment of heavenly realms, and the importance of donating to worthy Brahmins. The chapter also warns against reclaiming donated land.

beats.title: The Importance of Donations: Halapankti, Apakadana, and Grihadana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes various types of donations (dana) including halapankti (plow row) donation, apakadana (cooked food donation), and grihadana (house donation). It narrates the story of King Vanravahan and Queen Shubhavati who, through the guidance of sage Pippalada, performed apakadana to obtain children, ministers, and wealth. The chapter emphasizes the importance of donations, especially food and house donations, and their spiritual benefits.

beats.title: The Glory of Food Donation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the supreme importance of food donation (annadana). It narrates the story of King Shveta who, despite performing many sacrifices and attaining heaven, suffers from hunger because he never fed Brahmins properly. Following Brahma's advice, he feeds sage Agastya and is relieved. The chapter also tells of a prostitute named Dhaneshvara who raises a snake from an egg; the snake later threatens her son, but by feeding a thousand Brahmins, the snake is revived and the son saved. The sage Maitreya recounts how a poor Brahmin woman, by regularly donating rice pots (sthali), was reborn as Draupadi. The chapter concludes with praise of householders who honor women and feed guests.

beats.title: The Virtues of Maidservants and the Donation of Water Pavilions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana discusses the importance of having a virtuous maidservant (dasi) in the household, equating a home without milk, curd, ghee, and a maidservant to hell. It describes the characteristics of an ideal maidservant and the benefits of donating a maidservant to a Brahmin. The chapter then shifts to the construction and donation of a water pavilion (prapa) during the summer season, detailing the rituals, mantras, and rewards. It also covers the donation of a water pot (ghata) and the worship of the Ashvattha tree.

beats.title: The Merits of Donations: Fire, Books, and the Scale
beats.summary: This chapter of the Bhavishya Purana discusses the merits of various donations, focusing on the donation of fire (agniṣṭhikā dāna) during winter, the donation of books (vidyā dāna), and the detailed procedure for the tulā dāna (scale donation) as narrated by Pulastya Muni to King Trībindu. It emphasizes the spiritual benefits of these acts, including liberation from sins and attainment of heavenly realms.

beats.title: The Glory of Tula Purusha and Hiranyagarbha Donations
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals of Tula Purusha and Hiranyagarbha donations, their procedures, benefits, and the glory of these acts. It also includes a dialogue between King Yudhishthira and Lord Krishna about the highest forms of charity and austerity.

beats.title: The Glories of Bhuvana Pratishtha and Nakshatra Dana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the story of a king who performed a Brahmanda (cosmic egg) donation and attained Brahmaloka. It then details the ritual of Bhuvana Pratishtha (establishment of a cosmic diagram), including its construction, worship, and benefits. Finally, it explains the Nakshatra Dana (donations according to lunar mansions) and Tithi Dana (donations according to lunar days), with specific fruits for each.

beats.title: Donations on Tithis and Varaha-dana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the merits of various donations (dana) on specific tithis (lunar days), including gold, silver, and other items, and details the procedure for the Varaha-dana (boar donation) and the ten types of mountain donations (parvata-dana). It concludes with a dialogue between Yudhishthira and Krishna about the importance of these donations.

beats.title: The Merits of Donating Symbolic Mountains
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and merits of donating various symbolic mountains (parvatas) made of different substances: salt, jaggery, gold, sesame, cotton, and ghee. It includes the story of Queen Suchhama of King Marutta, who learned from sage Durvasa that her good fortune was due to a past life donation of a jaggery mountain. The chapter also recounts the origin of sesame from Vishnu's sweat during his battle with the demon Madhu, and prescribes the proper procedures for each donation, along with the associated rewards.

beats.title: The Merits of Donating Jewel and Silver Mountains and the Importance of Good Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana describes the merits of donating jewel mountains (ratnachala) and silver mountains (rajatachala), including the story of King Somatra and his queen Somavati, who were reborn due to a previous life's donation. It then transitions to a discourse on the importance of good conduct (sadachara) and the unity of deities, emphasizing that all gods are essentially one. The chapter concludes with instructions on daily rituals and proper behavior.

beats.title: Instructions on Right Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhavishya Purana (Chapter 4, part 7) contains instructions on proper conduct (sadachara) for householders, including rules about eating, bathing, sleeping, social interactions, and daily rituals. It emphasizes avoiding certain actions that decrease lifespan, such as disrespecting others, eating improperly, or associating with wicked people. The chapter concludes with Krishna advising King Yudhishthira on the importance of dharma and righteous living.

beats.title: Conclusion of Bhavishya Purana
beats.summary: This chapter concludes the Bhavishya Purana, emphasizing the merit of reciting and donating the Purana, especially on auspicious days. It recounts the instruction of dharma by Krishna to Yudhishthira and by Brahma to Yajnavalkya, and ends with a list of other texts published by the same press.
